content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

August 25, 1993


Ann Higginbothem
Assistant City Attorney
City of Pasadena
100 North Garfield Ave.
P.O. Box 7115
Pasadena, CA  91109-7215

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-93-301

Dear Ms. Higginbothem:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Pasadena Mayor Rick Cole, Councilmembers Chris Holden,
Isaac Richard, William Thomsen, City Manager Philip A. Hawkey, and
Assistant City Manager Edmund F. Sotelo, regarding their
responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

     Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.  In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.  The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

     Are Mayor Cole, Councilmembers Holden, Richard, Thomsen, City
Manager Hawkey, and Assistant City Manager Sotelo legally required
to participate in preparing a bid for the Superbowl in 1998 that
will be presented to the National Football League in October 1993?

CONCLUSION

     If it was not foreseeable at the time of the receipt of the
gifts from the National Football League that the city council

_________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA  95812 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-93-301
Page 2


would be making decisions affecting the National Football League
and if there exists no alternative source of decision, the
councilmembers are legally required to participate.

However, you have provided no facts to indicate that there
exists no alternative source of decision with respect to the city
manager and assistant city manager's participation in the
decision. If the participation of the city manager and assistant
city manager is not required by ordinance or other law, it would
not appear the exception in Regulation 18701 would apply.

FACTS

In 1993, Pasadena hosted the Superbowl of the National
Football League (the NFL). The NFL, in conjunction with the
Superbowl, provided gifts to the members of the city council and
city staff. You stated that Pasadena has seven councilmembers, of
those seven, four members received gifts in excess of $250. You
stated that the remaining three councilmembers have not received
gifts of $250 or more from the NFL.

On May 26, 1993, the National Football League informed the
City of Pasadena that under a newly implemented system, the
decision to award Superbowl XXXII (1998) made in October
1993. Pasadena has been invited to bid for this Superbowl. You
stated that prior to the notice received from the NFL, the city
council was not aware that they would be asked to make a bid for
the 1998 game within 12 months of the receipt of the gifts.

According to information you and Mr. James H. Steeg,
Executive Director for Special Events at the NFL, provided in our
telephone conversation of August 12, 1993, under the old system
the Superbowls were generally awarded a few years in advance in
bunches covering several future games. The old procedure was open
to more bidders and was generally less predictable as to when
games would be awarded. The new procedure was enacted to allow
planning in advance of the games, more so than under the past
system.

Thus, the city council must make a decision in September or
October of this year on whether to make a bid for this Superbowl,
and if so, what the terms of such bid will be. Such action will
require four votes. You have asked whether the disqualified
members are "legally required" to participate in the decision.

Additionally, two members of the city staff also received
gifts from the NFL aggregating $250 or more, the Pasadena City
Manager and the Assistant City Manager. You stated that the city
manager and the assistant city manager, in the normal course of
their duties, would be working on the preparation of the bid and
the negotiations with the NFL, as well as the presentation to the
NFL owners in October 1993 prior to the decision on the award of
the 1998 Superbowl.

Our File No. A-93-301
Page 3


ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.

Section 87103 provides that an official has a financial
interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is'reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a
donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating $250 or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

You stated that the National Football League provided gifts
in excess of $250 to the councilmembers in question. Thus, the
NFL is an economic interest of the councilmembers, and they are
prohibited from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using their official positions to influence a governmental
decision that will have a foreseeable² and material financial
effect on the NFL.

The Commission has adopted differing guidelines to determine
whether an effect is material, depending on the specific
circumstances of each decision. Where the donor of gifts is
directly before the city council, Regulation 18702.1(a) provides
that the effect of the decision is material and disqualification
is required.

A donor of gifts is directly before the city council when the
donor initiates the proceeding by filing an application, claim,
appeal, or similar request, or is a named party in the proceeding,
or the proceeding involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement
to, or contract with, the donor. (Regulation 18702.1(b).)

Under your facts, the NFL has asked Pasadena to participate
in the bidding for the Superbowl XXXII in 1998. The city
council's actions will directly affect the NFL.


2 Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case. An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur. Certainty is not required. However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In
re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Our File No. A-93-301
Page 4


However, Section 87101 provides a limited exception where the
official's participation is legally required:

     Section 87100 does not prevent any public of-
ficial from making or participating in the making
of a governmental decision to the extent his
participation is legally required for the action or
decision to be made.

                                        Section 87101.

     This exception has been narrowly interpreted to permit the
participation of the fewest financially interested persons pos-
sible in any decision. (In re Hudson (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 13; Hill
Advice Letter, No. I-89-160.)  Consequently, Regulation 18701
provides in pertinent part:

     (a)  A public official is not legally required
to make or to participate in the making of a
governmental decision within the meaning of Govern-
ment Code Section 87101 unless there exists no
alternative source of decision consistent with the
purposes and terms of the statute authorizing the
decision.

                         * * *

     (c)  This regulation shall be construed nar-
rowly, and shall:

          (1)  Not be construed to permit an of-
ficial, who is otherwise disqualified under
Government Code Section 87100, to vote to
break a tie.

          (2)  Not be construed to allow a member
of any public agency, who is otherwise
disqualified under Government Code Section
87100, to vote if a quorum can be convened of
other members of the agency who are not
disqualified under Government Code Section
87100, whether or not such other members are
actually present at the time of the
disqualification.

                              (Emphasis added.)

     We assume for purposes of this letter that only the city
council can act with respect to the decisions in question.

     Additionally, the Commission has determined that Section
87101 would not apply to a conflict of interest that arises due to
the receipt of gifts by an official if it was reasonably
foreseeable at the time the gifts were received that the official

Our File No. A-93-301
Page 5


would be asked to make or participate in the making of a
governmental decision affecting the donor. (In re Hopkins (1977)
3 FPPC Ops. 107.) The Commission stated:

     Therefore, we conclude that Section 87101 does
not apply to a conflict that arises because of
gifts an official has accepted if it was reasonably
foreseeable at the time the gift was received that
the official would be asked to make or participate
in the making of a governmental decision affecting
the donor.

This restriction on the application of Section 87101 was
further clarified in our advice letter to Jack L. White (Advice
Letter, No. O-86-001/A-86-143.) Mr. White requested advice on
behalf of Anaheim City Council with respect to a decision that
could materially affect Disneyland, and where Disneyland donated
gifts to the councilmembers. In that letter we stated:

     In the instant case, the gifts given by
Disneyland all occurred prior to the filing of the
subject application by a third party. The bulk of
the gifts giving rise to disqualification were
received last July, long before any knowledge of
this request existed. Consequently, the quoted
provisions set forth in the Hopkins Opinion are not
satisfied. Hence,...Section 87101 would apply to
these circumstances.

As stated above, an effect is considered reasonably
foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will
occur. Certainty is not required. However, if an effect is only
a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re
Thormer (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

City Councilmembers

Your facts are similar to those in the White situation.
Clearly, at the time that Pasadena hosted the 1993 Superbowl, it
was substantially likely that Pasadena would bid for the Superbowl
at some point in the future. This was also the case in the White
Advice Letter, since at the time the councilmembers received gift
passes it was substantially likely that the Anaheim City Council
would be making decisions affecting Disneyland in the future.

However, just as in the White Advice Letter, you stated it
was not foreseeable at the time that the Pasadena City
Councilmembers received the gifts from the NFL, that the NFL would
request a bid from Pasadena within 12 months of receiving the
gifts. While it appears that at the time the gifts were received
that it was possible that the proceeding involving the NFL would
have commenced within the 12 months specified in Section 87103(e)
(just as it was possible in the White Advice Letter), according to
your facts it was not substantially likely.

Our File No. A-93-301
Page 6

According to the information you and Mr. James H. Steeg,
Executive Director for Special Events at the NFL, provided in our
telephone conversation of August 12, 1993, the NFL notified
Pasadena (and other cities) of a new procedure used to select
Superbowl sites on May 26, 1993. Under the new procedure, the NFL
requested a bid from Pasadena for the 1998 Superbowl that would be
considered with other bids in October 1993.

Mr. Steeg stated that the new procedure was enacted to allow
planning in advance of the games, more so than under the past
system. Under the old system, the Superbowls were generally
awarded a few years in advance in bunches covering several future
games. The old procedure was open to more bidders, and Mr. Steeg
stated, generally less predictable.

Consequently, so long as it was not foreseeable at the time
of the receipt of the gifts from the NFL that the city council
would be making decisions affecting the NFL, the councilmembers
would be legally required to participate.

Please note, however, that "legally required participation"
applies only where disqualification, under Section 87100, has made
a quorum impossible. Additionally, the selected councilmember
must disclose, as a matter of official public record, the
existence of the financial interest; describe with particularity
the nature of the financial interest before he or she makes or
participates in making the decision; and, state the reason there
is no alternative source of decision-making authority
(Regulation 18701(b).)

Other City Officials

The same analysis with respect to foreseeability would appear
to apply to other city officials who received gifts from the NFL.
However, it does not appear from the facts that the officials in
question are legally required to make or to participate in the
making of the decision regarding the NFL.

In In re Maloney (1976) 3 FPPC Ops. 69, the Commission
discussed the issue as follows:

Another person may be appointed to act in his
stead in those instances where he has a conflict of
interest. Even in those cases where all Glenn
County qualified engineers or surveyors are
disabled from acting because of conflicts, there is
no need to apply the "legally required
participation" doctrine. It appears likely that it
would be a relatively simple matter to find a
qualified surveyor or engineer from an adjoining

Our File No. A-93-301
Page 7


county to provide the services which the Glenn
County engineers and surveyors cannot provide
because of disqualification.

* * *

Admittedly, use of this alternative works some
hardship on the county. We do not think, however,
that the concept of legally required participation
was included in the Political Reform Act merely to
alleviate the additional costs and inconveniences
associated with seeking an available alternative
source of decision. We think Section 87101's
application is limited to those situations where no
alternative source of decision exists and a failure
to make a decision will result in "a failure of
justice." Caminetti v. Pacific Mut. Life Ins. Co.,
supra. We conclude, therefore, that the county
surveyor-engineer is not "legally required" to act
in situations where he has a disqualifying conflict
of interest.

Applied to your facts, while it might be difficult to find
persons to act in the stead of the city manager and the assistant
city manager, it does not appear that there would exist no
alternative source of decision. Thus, unless their participation
is required by ordinance or other law, the exception would not
apply.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.³

Sincerely,

Wayne Ordos
Executive Director

By: John W. Wallace
    Counsel, Legal Division


³ Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
